sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Combat Application Tourniquet (CAT) is used for life-threatening external limb haemorrhage that is not controlled rapidly with direct pressure or where immediate control is required. Early tourniquet application can be lifesaving and should not be delayed while blood loss continues.

sections.title: Application
sections.content:
1. Expose the limb if practical and identify whether the bleeding is immediately life-threatening

2. Place the tourniquet 5-7 cm above the wound and not over a joint; if the wound is not visible or bleeding is extreme, place it high and tight on the limb

3. Pull the strap firmly tight before twisting the windlass

4. Twist the windlass until bleeding stops and distal bleeding is no longer seen; a palpable pulse may also disappear

5. Lock the windlass and secure the retaining strap

6. Record the application time on the tourniquet and in the clinical record

7. Reassess immediately; if bleeding continues, tighten further or apply a second tourniquet above the first

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Tourniquets are for life-threatening limb bleeding, not minor bleeding that can be controlled with simpler measures
- A correctly applied tourniquet is usually painful; pain alone is not a reason to loosen it
- Do not periodically release or remove a tourniquet in the prehospital setting
- If the first tourniquet is ineffective, apply a second rather than accepting partial control
- Continue overall haemorrhage, shock, and transport management after application
